claim: The eye puzzle is officially the 'Eye Messages': 9 messages of eye glyphs in the Parallel Worlds, considered to encode information but unsolved.
reasoning:
The structural facts of the claim are well-substantiated and corroborated across multiple independent sources, but the words "officially" and "encode information" are overstated relative to the actual evidence.

WHAT IS SUPPORTED (strong, multi-source):
1. The "9 messages" count and Parallel-World location. The Noita Wiki (wiki.gg, fetched via raw action) states verbatim: "The Eye Messages are a series of 9 messages that are found in East and West Parallel Worlds," with "4 messages generating in the western parallel world, and 5 messages generating in the eastern parallel world." This is independently corroborated by the SirCapybar GitHub research repo ("all of the 9 messages"; "unsolved for years now"), the Scribd/Google-Doc copy ("nine 'eye' messages... four located in West parallel worlds and five in East"), and the Steam discussion. The Google Doc title is confirmed as "Noita Eye Glyph Messages." Multiple converging community/data-mining sources, plus a reverse-engineered (Ninji/Ghidra) decompiled generator function, establish these are deterministic, seed-based, engine-generated artifacts — i.e., deliberately authored, not accidental. This is solid primary/near-primary evidence.
2. "Unsolved." Strongly supported and current as of 2026: the wiki, GitHub repo, Steam threads, and YouTube updates all agree no public, method-backed solution exists. Multiple "I solved the eyes" claims exist but none is accepted. This part is essentially confirmed.

WHAT IS OVERSTATED / NOT SUPPORTED:
1. "Officially." There is NO located developer (Petri Purho / Nolla Games) statement naming this feature "Eye Messages," confirming it is a puzzle, or confirming it encodes information. "Eye Messages" is a community-coined label; the wiki and repos use "eye messages / eye glyph puzzle / eye glyph messages" interchangeably with no cited official designation. Calling it "official" is unsubstantiated. (The closest thing to primary evidence of intent is the reverse-engineered generator code proving deliberate authorship — but that does not make the NAME or "puzzle" framing official.)
2. "Considered to encode information." The wiki's exact phrase is "They are known to contain information, but have yet to be solved." However, this "known to contain information" assertion is itself a community inference, NOT a developer confirmation. It rests on a statistical argument (only 1 of 36 reading orders yields an unbroken 0-82 base-10 range out of 0-124, claimed to be astronomically unlikely by chance) plus structural regularities (trigrams, isomorphs, 83 distinct values). The wiki elsewhere hedges: the purpose "is currently unknown, but it is strongly believed that they are a puzzle." So "known to contain information" is presented as fact on the wiki but is actually a strongly-held community belief backed by statistics, not a primary confirmation — and notably, frequency analysis on trigrams has failed, so even the "encoded message" model is unproven.

NET: The factual scaffolding (9 messages, 4 west / 5 east, Parallel Worlds, currently unsolved, deliberately generated) is well-supported. The framing words ("officially," "encode information" stated as established fact) are not backed by any primary developer source and rest on community consensus/statistical inference. Hence "mixed" rather than "supported." I could not access the live Google Doc body (403/400/redirect blocks) or the wiki.gg HTML (self-signed cert) directly, but obtained the wiki content via the raw API and the doc's count via three independent mirrors, so the core facts are confirmed despite those access failures.

claim: 9 eye messages total: 5 East, 4 West. Internally placed alternating E,W,E,W...; every East message except the last (East 5) has a West counterpart in the same location. (Messages numbered 0-8, Message 8 = East 5, the one with no West counterpart.)
reasoning:
Every element of the claim is confirmed verbatim against the cited primary source and independently corroborated by two further sources.

PRIMARY SOURCE (the cited Google Doc, title "Noita Eye Messages", last updated 2021-03-25, reverse-engineering credited to @Ninji — a well-known Noita reverse engineer). I retrieved the full verbatim text. It states:
- "There are nine messages that can be displayed via 'eye' glyphs. Four of these are in West parallel worlds, five in East worlds." → 9 total, 5 East / 4 West. EXACT MATCH.
- "Internally, the messages are placed in East, West, East, West, etc. in alternating order." → EXACT MATCH to the alternating-placement claim.
- Numbering 0-8: it lists "Message 0 (East 1), Message 1 (West 1), Message 2 (East 2), Message 3 (West 2), Message 4 (East 3), Message 5 (West 3), Message 6 (East 4), Message 7 (West 4), Message 8 (East 5)." → confirms the 0-8 numbering and that Message 8 = East 5. EXACT MATCH.
- "Message 8 is the only one that does not have a corresponding message in West." and "Every eye message, except the last one in East, has a corresponding message in the opposing parallel world." → directly confirms "every East message except the last (East 5) has a West counterpart." EXACT MATCH.

So the claim is not just a fair paraphrase; it is a near-verbatim restatement of the primary document, including the numbering nuance.

INDEPENDENT CORROBORATION:
1. The Noita Wiki (noita.wiki.gg / Fandom mirror) independently states 9 total, 4 West / 5 East, counterpart in opposite parallel world "with one exception that has no western counterpart," and even supplies a concrete reproducible example: seed 1249563923 with East 1-5 and West 1-4, East 5 having no West counterpart. This is reproducible in-game observation, not just a copy of the doc.
2. The GitHub research repo (ngraham20/NoitaCryptographyResearch) independently lists the same 9-item East1, West1, East2, West2, East3, West3, East4, West4, East5 sequence and explicitly notes "there is no West 5."

EVIDENCE QUALITY / HONEST CAVEAT: The structural claim (counts, alternation, missing West counterpart) is well-substantiated and NOT merely repeated lore. It is verifiable three ways: (a) Ninji's Ghidra decompilation of "the decompiled function that generates the messages," (b) a Cheat Engine Lua script that prints the generated eye coordinates per visited East/West biome, and (c) per-seed in-game coordinate tables on the wiki. The one nuance worth flagging: the generation code is hardcoded in the engine (NOT a readable Lua script and not extractable from the data archive), so this is "reproducible-observation + decompilation" evidence rather than open game source. That does not weaken the specific structural claim here, which is consistent across all independent sources with zero contradicting evidence found. The deeper meaning/decryption of the messages remains unsolved, but the claim at issue is purely about message count and placement, which is firmly established. Verdict: supported.

claim: Each Noita eye-puzzle glyph is one of five orientations encoded as digits 0-4; the digit '5' creates a new line of eyes and is not displayed. Cited source: the "Noita Eye Glyph Messages" Google Doc (docs.google.com/document/d/1s6gxrc1iLJ78iFfqC2d4qpB9_r_c5U5KwoHVYFFrjy0).
reasoning:
VERBATIM QUOTE IS REAL. The exact sentence "The '5' character creates a new line of eyes and is not actually displayed otherwise. The game converts these strings of numbers into eye glyphs using the following table:" appears in the cited Google Doc and is mirrored verbatim on the Noita Wiki (noita.wiki.gg and Fandom) Eye Messages page, plus the Scribd/techhenzy copies. Search-engine snippets returned this sentence consistently across all these pages.

FIVE ORIENTATIONS / DIGITS 0-4 IS CORROBORATED AND INTERNALLY CONSISTENT. Multiple independent sources state "Individual eye glyphs are in one of five orientations" (confirmed directly in the raw wiki.gg page text). The conversion table is described as having columns 0,1,2,3,4 (five orientations). This is mathematically consistent with the universally-accepted base-5 trigram model: trigrams range 000-444 = 5x5x5 = 125 possible values (decimal 0-124), with ~83 used. The ngraham20 GitHub research shows trigram data composed only of digits 0-2/etc. with decimals up to 82 - exactly what base-5 (digits 0-4) produces. The role of '5' as a separate control digit (newline) explains why glyph orientations span only 0-4: '5' is not a glyph orientation at all.

THIS IS PRIMARY-GROUNDED, NOT MERELY REPEATED LORE - the key reason for 'supported' rather than 'mixed'. The number strings and the encoding scheme are NOT community speculation: they originate from reverse-engineering of the actual game binary. Ninji (a known reverse engineer) produced a Ghidra project decompiling the engine function that generates the eye messages; the wiki/community explicitly attribute the data to "@Ninji ... reverse engineering work" with save-file contributions. The eye-spawning code is engine-side (explicitly noted as "not a Lua script" and not extractable from the data archive), which is precisely why the 0-4-orientations + '5'=newline behavior is described as how the game's own code converts the number strings - i.e. derived from decompiled code, the strongest available primary evidence short of dev confirmation.

CAVEATS / WHAT I COULD NOT FULLY VERIFY DIRECTLY: (1) The Google Doc body and the wiki's conversion-table IMAGE could not be loaded directly (Google Docs returned only JS navigation; wiki.gg gave a self-signed-cert error on full HTML; Fandom returned 403). I confirmed the verbatim sentence and the five-orientations statement via repeated search-engine snippets and the raw wiki source, plus base-5 math, rather than rendering the table image pixel-by-pixel. (2) I did not personally open Ninji's Ghidra decompilation to read the literal switch/branch handling digit 5, so the decompilation is reported via secondary description; however the attribution to a Ghidra-based decompiled message-generation function is consistently and specifically stated. (3) The puzzle's MEANING remains unsolved - but the claim is only about the low-level encoding (orientations + newline), which is the well-established, primary-grounded part. Given the verbatim match to the cited source AND grounding in decompiled game code, this is genuinely substantiated, not just widely repeated.

claim: The specific direction-per-digit mapping for Noita eye glyphs is 0=neutral/center, 1=up, 2=right, 3=down, 4=left.
reasoning:
No primary source pins each pixel-direction to its digit, and multiple lines of evidence actively undercut treating this specific ordering as established fact.

1) NO EXTRACTABLE PRIMARY SOURCE EXISTS. This is the most important finding. Both noita.wiki.gg and the community primary doc repeatedly state that the eye messages are rendered directly by the game ENGINE, not by a Lua script, and have no associated sprite files: "The code that spawns the eyes is not a Lua script and cannot be extracted from the archive... the messages are rendered using the engine, and do not have any sprites associated with them." Reverse-engineering would require Ghidra/disassembly. Consequently there is no retrievable code, sprite-UV table, or data file that authoritatively defines digit -> direction. The claim therefore cannot be grounded in primary game data through any normally accessible route. (Note: a separate community statement that "the game converts strings of numbers into eye glyphs using a table 0 1 2 3 4" refers to the existence of five values, NOT to a documented direction assignment.)

2) THE WIKI MAPPING IS IMAGE-ONLY. Confirmed by fetching the wiki.gg page's wikitext: the five distinct orientations are presented solely via an image file (Eyeorientation.png). The wikitext contains the orientation-to-value correspondence only in that image, never as text. So even the secondary/community source cannot be cited as a text statement supporting the specific 0/1/2/3/4 ordering; it can only be read off a sprite render, which (per the task constraint) I could not directly verify pixel-by-pixel.

3) NO AUTHORITATIVE SOURCE EXPLICITLY STATES THE 0=center,1=up,2=right,3=down,4=left ORDERING. Every page checked (noita.wiki.gg Eye Messages and Template:Glyph_Translation, Fandom, Cipherbrain/scienceblogs, Steam guide 3281214266, the ngraham20 GitHub research repo README, CinderLordTurtle translator repo, Scribd primary doc) describes "five orientations / pupil at top, bottom, left, right, center = 0-4" but NONE states which orientation equals which specific digit in text. Targeted searches for the exact ordering returned nothing.

4) DIRECT CONTRADICTING SIGNAL ON THE ORDER. The Cipherbrain analysis explicitly warns that the assignment of the five eye symbols to numbers 0-4 is non-obvious: "the five eye symbols are represented by the numbers 0 to 4. The order is different from the two pictures." This is direct evidence that the specific ordering is not self-evident and that naive direction orderings can be wrong, which undercuts confident assertion of any one mapping (including the claimed one).

5) THE CLAIMED MAPPING IS ALSO NOT THE COMMUNITY'S OPERATIVE VARIABLE. The community's accepted convention is about TRIGRAM READING ORDER (the single reading order out of 36 that yields an unbroken base-10 range 0-82), not about a canonical direction-per-digit legend. The base-5 digit-to-orientation legend is essentially an arbitrary labeling choice; the puzzle remains unsolved regardless. So the claim conflates an arbitrary relabeling with substantiated cryptographic structure.

Why not 'refuted': nothing I found proves the specific ordering is wrong; an internally consistent base-5 relabeling can be adopted by convention, and the image-based legend may well correspond to it. Why not 'mixed'/'supported': the parts that ARE true (five orientations; values 0-4 exist; base-5 trigrams) do not touch the specific per-digit direction assignment, which has NO retrievable text/primary basis and is contradicted in spirit by Cipherbrain's "the order is different" caveat. Net: the specific 0=center,1=up,2=right,3=down,4=left mapping is widely repeatable as an arbitrary convention but is not substantiated by any verifiable primary or strong secondary text source -> unverifiable.

claim: Noita eye-message glyphs are arranged in rows (max 39 per row) with every second row offset to interlock between neighbors.
reasoning:
Two sub-claims must be separated.

(1) "Every second/other row is offset to interlock/mesh between neighbors" — WELL SUPPORTED (high confidence). This is not merely repeated lore; it is a directly observable, reproducible visual property of the actual in-game messages. The eyes are packed in a hexagonal/honeycomb arrangement where alternate rows sit in the gaps of their neighbors, and this is visible in wiki screenshots of real messages (e.g., the "East 1" message image). The wiki states the messages are rendered by the engine with no associated sprites, i.e. procedurally generated, so the offset is an intrinsic layout property anyone can observe. The claim's quoted wiki text ("Every second row is offset to fit in-between the neighboring rows") and the primary-doc phrasing ("every other line offset slightly to mesh together") match verbatim and describe the same observable feature. Multiple independent community efforts (Toboter/ToboterXP, Lymm, codewarrior0, SirCapybar, lastCoyotes, ngraham20) all work from transcriptions of these same physically-arranged messages.

(2) "At most 39 individual eyes in each row" — SUPPORTED ONLY AS AN OBSERVED MAXIMUM, NOT A PROVEN HARD LIMIT (medium confidence). The "39" is the empirically observed longest row across the 9 transcribed messages, repeated consistently across the wiki and the primary doc. I found NO primary evidence that 39 is a hardcoded engine constant (no decompiled/source value cited) and NO developer/Nolla statement confirming it. The wiki itself frames it descriptively ("with at most 39"), and notes the bottom two rows are shorter and vary, indicating 39 is an observed/derived bound rather than a documented cap. So the specific number is an inference from the small fixed sample of 9 messages, which could in principle differ; it is widely repeated but rests on community transcription rather than verified game-data.

CRITICAL CAVEAT ON THE SOURCE CHAIN: The cited "primary doc" is the community document "Noita Eye Glyph Messages" (circulated via Google Docs and mirrored on Scribd/Course Hero), authored/maintained by the secret-hunting community, NOT an official Nolla/dev artifact. The wiki text appears largely derived from this same community doc, so wiki and "primary doc" are not fully independent sources — they share an origin. The offset/interlock fact survives this because it is independently re-observable in-game; the "39" figure is more dependent on that shared community transcription. Hence: the interlocking-offset structure is substantiated, but the precise "max 39 per row" is an observation presented with more certainty than its evidence base strictly warrants — yielding an overall MIXED verdict.

claim: Trigrams: groups of three eyes read as a 3-digit base-5 number (000-444 = 0-124). EVERY message's eye count is divisible by three. Per-message eye counts {East1:297, West1:309, East2:354, West2:306, East3:411, West3:372, East4:357, West4:360, East5:342} are all divisible by 3. The trigram grouping was proposed by Reddit user The_Duck1.
reasoning:
CORE NUMERIC CLAIM: SUPPORTED by primary data. I independently reproduced this from the raw eye sequences in the community research repo (ngraham20/NoitaCryptographyResearch, file eye/eyes.json), which store each message as a string of digits 0-4 (eye orientations) with the digit 5 used as a DELIMITER (eyes.json field "delimiter":5). When the delimiter is excluded, the actual eye counts are EXACTLY {east1:297, west1:309, east2:354, west2:306, east3:411, west3:372, east4:357, west4:360, east5:342} and ALL NINE are divisible by 3. IMPORTANT QUALIFIER the claim missed: the raw stored string LENGTHS (including delimiters) are 305/317/364/314/423/382/367/370/352 and are mostly NOT divisible by 3 (only east3 is). So divisibility holds ONLY when counting actual eyes, not the row/section delimiters. The "eye count divisible by three" wording is corroborated verbatim by the Noita Wiki (wiki.gg): "Every message has a total number of eye glyphs that is divisible by three."

TRIGRAM / BASE-5 MECHANISM: SUPPORTED but with a critical caveat the claim understates. I verified from the primary trigram CSV (eye/reference/noita_eye_data_trigrams.csv) and the program README that each message's trigram count equals eyes/3 and that across ALL nine messages the trigram values form the UNBROKEN set 0-82 (83 distinct values), not the naive 0-124. Crucially, a NAIVE left-to-right group-of-three base-5 reading does NOT reproduce the data (it yields values up to 122 and does not match the CSV). The accepted result requires a specific paired-row reading order (the README's "200"->50 etc. matches the CSV). So "groups of three eyes read as a 3-digit base-5 number" is mechanically true, but the meaningful 0-82 structure depends on one specific non-trivial reading order selected out of ~36/~86,000 candidates (Toboter's script), a fact the claim's framing glosses over. The 0-124 figure is only the theoretical max; the actual used range is 0-82.

THE_DUCK1 ATTRIBUTION: NOT SUBSTANTIATED / UNVERIFIABLE. This is where the claim is weakest. The directly-fetched wiki.gg article does NOT mention "The_Duck1" anywhere; it credits Lymm, Pyry, Toboter, and CodeWarrior0 (Toboter for the ~86,000 reading-order script). The research repo contains no mention of "duck". The The_Duck1 attribution appears ONLY inside AI-generated search-result summaries, which I could not confirm against any directly-fetched primary page (no original Reddit thread located; Fandom returned HTTP 403). So the trigram idea being "proposed by Reddit user The_Duck1" is repeated lore that I could not verify against a primary source, and the most authoritative source I could read credits different people.

OVERALL: The numeric facts (counts, divisibility-by-3-of-eyes, 83-value 0-82 trigram set) are PRIMARY-verified and true. The attribution sub-claim and the implied simplicity of the base-5 grouping are not well supported. Net verdict: mixed.
sources: https://github.com/ngraham20/NoitaCryptographyResearch (cloned; primary data: eye/eyes.json with field 'delimiter':5, eye/reference/noita_eye_data_trigrams.csv, eye/README.md, eye/TASKS.md, root README.md showing trigram->decimal mapping e.g. '200'->50)

https://noita.wiki.gg/wiki/Eye_Messages (states verbatim 'Every message has a total number of eye glyphs that is divisible by three'; describes trigram base-5 0-124; '0-82 unbroken' reading order; credits Lymm, Pyry, Toboter, CodeWarrior0; does NOT mention The_Duck1)
https://noita.fandom.com/wiki/Eye_Messages (mirror; returned HTTP 403 on direct raw fetch, only available via search snippets)
https://steamcommunity.com/app/881100/discussions/0/4700161534027181070/ (Eye Messages discussion; references Lymm and codewarrior0 and trigrams/Google Docs; no The_Duck1, no divisibility statement)
https://www.scribd.com/document/911932819/Noita-Eye-Glyph-Messages ('Noita Eye Messages Explained' primary write-up; full body not extractable without login)

claim: Total trigram count across all 9 Noita eye messages is exactly 1036, matching the published probability figure (83/125)^1036 = 5.836e-185.
reasoning:
TWO SUB-CLAIMS, evaluated separately against primary evidence.

(1) "Total trigrams = 1036" — STRONGLY SUPPORTED (high confidence). I independently reproduced this from primary data, not just repeated lore. The Noita Wiki cites user Toboter's bruteforce script; I fetched that script's raw glyph data (the `eyeTris` array in https://raw.githubusercontent.com/ToboterXP/EyeGlyphs/main/archive/eyeGlyphs-trigram%20order%20bruteforce.py). It contains 9 messages with glyph counts 297,309,354,306,411,372,357,360,342 = 3108 total glyphs, each divisible by 3, giving 99+103+118+102+137+124+119+120+114 = 1036 trigrams. The alternate `eyeNorm` array also yields 1036. GitHub repo ngraham20/NoitaCryptographyResearch and the wiki both independently confirm there are exactly 9 messages (East 1-5 and West 1-4). So 1036 is real and reproducible.

(2) "matching the published probability figure (83/125)^1036 = 5.836e-185" — PARTIALLY SUPPORTED, with a genuine defect. The figure IS published verbatim on the primary source (noita.wiki.gg/wiki/Eye_Messages): "(83/125)^1036 = 5.8362007929568295 * 10^-185" plus a 'grain of sand' analogy. The exponent (-185) and ~12 leading significant figures are correct. BUT the exact mantissa the wiki publishes is WRONG: high-precision computation gives (83/125)^1036 = 5.836200792956514e-185, i.e. trailing digits ...6514, whereas the wiki states ...68295 (a float64 rounding artifact). So the claim's assertion that the figure is the literal published value is true, but the published value itself is imprecise beyond ~12 sig figs.

CAVEATS / FLAGS: (a) The wiki does NOT show the per-message breakdown deriving 1036 — that number is asserted, not derived, on the page; I had to go to Toboter's raw data to substantiate it. (b) Minor inconsistency: the formula writes denominator 125 while text says 0-124 = 125 possible and 0-82 = 83 used (5^3=125; internally consistent, just a labeling quirk). (c) This is all about the STATISTICAL ARGUMENT that the orthodox reading order is non-random; it does NOT mean the puzzle is solved — it remains unsolved. (d) 1036 trigrams is a property of the orthodox grouping; it is interpretation-dependent, though the divisible-by-3 glyph counts are an objective in-game fact.

Net: the trigram count of 1036 is solidly verified; the probability constant is published as claimed but its quoted mantissa is numerically slightly inaccurate — hence MIXED rather than fully supported.

claim: The eye-spawning code is built into the Noita engine (not a Lua script) and cannot be extracted from the game archive; the eye graphics are not stored as assets but generated on the fly.
reasoning:
PROVENANCE OF THE QUOTE: The verbatim claim ('the code that spawns the eyes is not a Lua script and cannot be extracted from the archive. It is built into the engine itself. The graphics are also not in the archive but are generated on the fly') appears word-for-word on the Noita Wiki (noita.wiki.gg and the Fandom mirror) and in the cited 'Noita Eye Glyph Messages' Google Doc / its Scribd copy. So the cited source genuinely contains the statement, and it is widely repeated. The Google Doc and Scribd page bodies were not directly readable (Docs returned only chrome; Scribd returned only metadata; noita.wiki.gg returns a self-signed-cert error to the fetcher and Fandom returns 403), so I relied on multiple corroborating search-index snapshots of the same wiki text.

INDEPENDENT SUBSTANTIATION (the important part): The spawn-code half of the claim is not merely repeated lore — it is backed by independent technical reverse engineering. The wiki and community materials credit the reverse-engineer Ninji (Ash Wolf / 'Treeki', a well-known, credible RE figure) with partially DISASSEMBLING the game and producing a downloadable Ghidra project (~60 MB) containing the decompiled function that generates the eye messages. A decompiled NATIVE function in the engine binary is direct evidence the logic is compiled into the engine, not present as a Lua script inside data.wak. This is consistent, corroborating primary-style evidence rather than circular citation. Further, the only known programmatic way the community can locate eyes is a Cheat Engine Lua script that hooks the RUNNING process and prints the generated coordinates (and must be re-patched each game update) — exactly what you would expect if eyes are generated at runtime by engine code reading the seed, not stored as data. I found NO contradicting evidence: nobody reports finding eye-spawn Lua scripts or eye sprites by unpacking data.wak (which IS otherwise fully unpackable — Nolla even documents how, and most sprites live in data.wak). The absence of eye assets there, combined with the decompile, supports the engine-level claim.

QUALIFICATIONS / WEAKER PARTS: (1) No developer/Nolla/Petri Purho statement confirms any of this; it is entirely community reverse engineering — credible, but not first-party. (2) The 'graphics are generated on the fly / no sprites' half is repeated by the same wiki/community sources and is plausible (glyphs appear to be line-drawn procedurally by the engine), but I did not find an independent primary artifact specifically documenting the rendering path the way the Ghidra decompile documents the spawn/generation function. So the rendering claim is consistent and uncontradicted but rests more on community assertion than the spawn-code claim does. (3) I could not load the cited Google Doc body itself to confirm what evidence IT provides; its authority is as a community compilation, not a primary source. Net: the spawn-code-in-engine / not-extractable claim is well supported by independent RE; the on-the-fly-graphics claim is supported but on weaker, less independently verified footing — hence 'supported' overall with the graphics caveat, not 'mixed', since no part is contradicted and the central technical assertion is corroborated.

claim: The Noita eye-message CONTENT was obtained via Cheat Engine to locate eyes per seed, and via Ninji's partial disassembly (a downloadable Ghidra project, with a viewable decompiled message-generating function), which nuances the "cannot be data-mined" claim: the engine asset archive can't yield it, but reverse engineering of the binary did.
reasoning:
PARTIALLY SUPPORTED, with two material qualifications and one likely overstatement.

WHAT IS SUPPORTED (by the cited primary doc):
1. The cited Google Doc (docs.google.com/document/d/1s6gxrc1iLJ78iFfqC2d4qpB9_r_c5U5KwoHVYFFrjy0) does contain the quoted text almost verbatim. Plain-text export (via the /export?format=txt redirect) yields: "Ninji ... did some reverse-engineering. If you're familiar with Ghidra, you can download the Ghidra project (60 MB). You may also view the decompiled function that generates the messages. There is also another document about reverse-engineering Noita." So the stated evidence is faithfully transcribed from the source.
2. The data-mining nuance is genuinely in the source: "Note that the code that spawns the eyes is not a Lua script and cannot be extracted from the archive. It is built into the engine itself. The graphics are also not in the archive but are generated on the fly." This corroborates the claim's framing (archive can't yield it; binary RE can).
3. The Cheat Engine method is corroborated by both the doc and an independent Steam community thread: a Cheat Engine Lua script triggers in East/West biomes and prints generated eye coordinates per seed (a given seed always spawns eyes in the same places). So "locate eyes per seed via Cheat Engine" is well-attested.
4. Ninji is a real, prominent reverse engineer (wuffs.org; known for Animal Crossing: New Horizons and Super Mario Maker RE), so the attribution is plausible and internally consistent.

QUALIFICATION 1 (sourcing is thin / largely single-source): I could NOT independently verify the actual 60 MB Ghidra project file or the decompiled function as a live, accessible primary artifact. Ninji's own website (wuffs.org/projects and wuffs.org/blog) has NO Noita project page or blog post. The plain-text doc export shows the resources are only described ("linked at the top of the document") with no resolvable URLs surfaced, and the Cheat Engine script author is even left as a placeholder "[todo: name here]". The current authoritative Noita Wiki (noita.wiki.gg/wiki/Eye_Messages) and the NoitaCryptographyResearch GitHub repo do NOT mention Ninji or Ghidra at all. So this is essentially a single-community-document claim, not a multiply-corroborated, primary-artifact-verified fact.

QUALIFICATION/OVERSTATEMENT 2 (the headline word "CONTENT" is misleading): The claim says the message CONTENT was obtained. The evidence shows reverse engineering revealed the GENERATION ALGORITHM and glyph-placement (how the 0-5 values are produced and laid out), and Cheat Engine reveals LOCATIONS/coordinates. It did NOT yield a decrypted plaintext meaning. Multiple sources (noita.wiki.gg, the crypto research repo, FuryForged) state the messages remain UNSOLVED with no public successful translation. The "decompiled function that generates the messages" produces the displayed eye layout, not a human-readable decrypted message. So "content was obtained" is true only in the narrow sense of "the raw glyph sequence per seed is recoverable," and false in the sense of "the meaning/plaintext was extracted."

Net: the specific cited evidence is accurately quoted and the data-mining nuance is real (mixed leaning supported on the narrow technical claim), but it rests on a single community doc I could not back with the actual artifacts, and the word CONTENT overstates what was achieved (glyph sequences/locations and the generator, not a solved/decrypted message). Hence MIXED.

claim: Noita eye messages spawn conditions: (1) the mods_have_been_active_during_this_run flag must not be set; (2) the player must have triggered the "Entered East/West" message; (3) eye messages only spawn at locations with background background_cave_02.png; (4) a given seed always spawns them in the same locations (seed-deterministic).
reasoning:
All four sub-claims trace back to a single identifiable PRIMARY source — the "Noita Eye Messages" Google Doc (last updated 2021-03-25), based on reverse-engineering of the game by @Ninji and maintained by @Xkeeper. I retrieved the doc's actual body text via the plain-text export endpoint, which confirms each clause essentially verbatim: "For the eyes to appear, the 'mods_have_been_active_during_this_run' flag must not be set."; "They will not appear until the 'Entered East/West' message is triggered, even if they would otherwise be visible."; "The game will only spawn the eye messages in locations with the background 'background_cave_02.png'..."; and "A given seed will always spawn them in the same locations." This is not merely repeated lore — it derives from code/data-level reverse engineering, and the noita.wiki.gg Eye Messages page reproduces the same conditions.

Independent corroboration strengthens the verdict rather than just echoing it: the mods flag is a genuine, well-documented game-save attribute. Multiple Steam Community discussions and wiki pages describe editing mods_have_been_active_during_this_run="1" to "0" in world_state.xml (AppData/LocalLow/Nolla_Games_Noita/save00) as the known workaround to restore eye-message generation, and the broader Mod Restrictions mechanic (eye messages + Cauldron/gods messages disabled when any non-translation mod is active) is established. So sub-claim (1) is independently verifiable. Sub-claims (2), (3) and (4) currently rest primarily on the same Ninji/Xkeeper reverse-engineering lineage rather than two fully independent primary sources, but that lineage IS primary (derived from the game's own data, not folklore) and is uncontested.

Adversarial check: I searched specifically for contradicting or dissenting views and found none refuting any of the four conditions — the community is uniform on them; the open/unsolved part is the MEANING of the glyphs and the exact seed-to-coordinate generation algorithm, not these spawn gating conditions. Minor qualifications (do not change the verdict): (a) the background condition is the single file "background_cave_02.png" and includes some invisible-background areas (EDR walls, Lake area) per the source's own note; (b) "seed-deterministic" is accurate for spawn LOCATIONS, while the underlying generation method remains not fully reverse-engineered; (c) at least one secondary restatement adds "this condition resets when the player closes the game," which is an extra detail not part of the original four claims and which I would treat as lower-confidence. None of these contradict the claim as stated. Given a real, retrievable primary source plus independent verification of the most consequential clause, this rates as supported rather than mixed.
sources: https://docs.google.com/document/d/1s6gxrc1iLJ78iFfqC2d4qpB9_r_c5U5KwoHVYFFrjy0 (primary source; body text retrieved via /export?format=txt — 'Noita Eye Messages', updated 2021-03-25, reverse engineering by @Ninji / maintained by @Xkeeper)
https://noita.wiki.gg/wiki/Eye_Messages (reproduces all four spawn conditions verbatim)
https://noita.fandom.com/wiki/Eye_Messages (mirror of the same conditions; returned 403 on direct fetch but indexed in search)
https://noita.wiki.gg/wiki/Mod_Restrictions (documents eye messages disabled when mods active; the second 'no-mods-only' secret)
https://steamcommunity.com/app/881100/discussions/0/5169559267834866364/ ('what content will not show up if you use mods' — confirms eye messages gated by mods)
https://github.com/noita-player/noitadocs/wiki/Reversing-data.wak (context on the data.wak reverse-engineering that underpins the primary doc)
https://noita.wiki.gg/wiki/Mysteries_and_Oddities (eye puzzle still unsolved re: meaning, not spawn conditions)

claim: Example seed 1249563923 gives specific eye-message coordinates (East1 X:22064 Y:-6079, West1 X:-49616 Y:-6079; East5 X:36400 Y:2624 with no Western counterpart), per noita.wiki.gg/wiki/Eye_Messages. Not re-verified in-game; primary doc warns the Cheat Engine script must be updated per Noita version, so coordinates may shift across versions.
reasoning:
COORDINATE VALUES — SUPPORTED (strong documentary match). I retrieved the FULL primary wikitext of noita.wiki.gg/wiki/Eye_Messages directly via the MediaWiki API (action=parse, pageid 4291), bypassing wiki.gg's anti-bot block. The page contains an "Example Coordinates" table for Seed 1249563923 whose values match the claim EXACTLY: East row 1 = X:22064 Y:-6079, West row 1 = X:-49616 Y:-6079; East row 5 = X:36400 Y:2624 with an empty West cell. Full table: E2 X:52272 Y:14400 / W2 X:-19408 Y:14400; E3 X:52784 Y:-5055 / W3 X:-18896 Y:-5055; E4 X:52784 Y:6208 / W4 X:-18896 Y:6208. So the cited claim transcribes the source accurately, and the "East5 has no Western counterpart" detail is corroborated both by the empty cell and by the page's prose ("4 messages in the western parallel world, and 5 in the eastern... with one exception that has no western counterpart").

ONE FRAMING CAVEAT: the claim's shorthand "East1/West1" is slightly ambiguous. In the source, the bold row labels 1-5 are MESSAGE INDICES within a parallel world, while "East/West" are the column headers (parallel-world side). A WebSearch summarizer separately misread "East 1/West 1" as the parallel-world NAMES (the nearest East/West parallel worlds are literally called "East 1"/"West 1" in Noita). The claim's intent (message #1 in the East PW) is correct and matches the table, but the notation collides with Noita's parallel-world naming and could mislead.

DETERMINISM / "may shift across versions" — PARTIALLY MISLEADING. Eye-message spawn locations are computed deterministically from the world seed by Noita's worldgen; Lymm's standalone Python script (gitlab.com/realgonzogames/lymms-binoculars) and its successor noita-telescope reproduce coordinates from a seed WITHOUT running the game or Cheat Engine, which is itself evidence the values are reproducible rather than a one-off in-game capture. Coordinates can in principle change if Nolla alters worldgen between versions (true of any seed-derived Noita data), but that is a generic caveat, not a documented instance of these specific coordinates shifting.

CHEAT-ENGINE WARNING — MISATTRIBUTED (the main refutation). The claim says the cited primary doc (noita.wiki.gg) "explicitly warns the Cheat Engine script must be updated per Noita version." The full current noita.wiki.gg page text I retrieved does NOT mention Cheat Engine at all; its "Finding the Eye Messages" section instead cites Lymm's Python script and Chillie's web tool. The "script you can run in Cheat Engine's Lua Engine... when Noita updates, the script needs to be updated" wording appears on the FANDOM mirror (noita.fandom.com/wiki/Eye_Messages), a different page. So the version-dependency warning is real, but it is sourced to Fandom, not to the URL the claim cites — the stated evidence conflates two pages.

NOT INDEPENDENTLY RE-VERIFIED IN-GAME. I could not (and the claim concedes it did not) launch Noita on the current version to confirm glyphs actually spawn at these coordinates for seed 1249563923. So the underlying in-game fact rests on community documentation, not a fresh reproducible observation. Net: coordinate transcription is well-substantiated against the primary wiki source; the surrounding evidentiary framing (Cheat Engine warning attributed to wiki.gg, plus the lack of in-game re-verification) is partly inaccurate — hence MIXED rather than SUPPORTED.

claim: Eye Messages and the Cauldron Room are the two 'major secrets' visible only in mod-free runs / the most popular unsolved mysteries. Stated source: noita.wiki.gg/wiki/Mysteries_and_Oddities verbatim says 'Among the most popular unsolved mysteries are the Eye Messages and Cauldron Room'; the 'two major secrets / mod-free' framing is from the Eye Messages page; Epilogue 2 (2024) is widely read as resolving the Cauldron.
reasoning:
I verified the wiki text directly against the live primary source (fetched via curl because noita.wiki.gg presents a self-signed certificate that blocked the standard fetch tool, and the Fandom mirror returned HTTP 403).

WHAT IS FULLY SUPPORTED (verbatim, exact match):
1. Mysteries_and_Oddities page intro reads VERBATIM: "Noita has many unsolved mysteries and miscellaneous oddities that have no known association. Among the most popular unsolved mysteries are the Eye Messages and Cauldron Room." This exactly matches the claim, including that this page does NOT rank them as 'the two major mod-free secrets' — it only calls them 'the most popular unsolved mysteries.'
2. The 'two major secrets / mod-free' framing does indeed come from the Eye Messages page, VERBATIM: "They are one of two major secrets in Noita that can only be seen when playing without any mods enabled. The other is the Cauldron Room." The claim correctly attributes this wording to the Eye Messages page, not the Mysteries page.
3. The Epilogue 2 caveat is supported by the Cauldron Room page VERBATIM: "In 2024 the Epilogue 2 update released, retroactively tying the cauldron into the Mr. Olli's Wild Ride questline, this is often interpreted as the Cauldron being given its functionality, a closing of the book on this unsolved mystery." So 'two unsolved secrets is now arguably one' is well-grounded in the wiki's own current text.

WHY MIXED RATHER THAN SUPPORTED — internal contradiction and weak primary basis for the 'two / only-without-mods' claim:
A. The 'two secrets' count is NOT consistent across the wiki. The Cauldron Room page itself states there are "one of three proper 'secrets' that are hidden in this manner [Mod Restrictions], including the only confirmed unsolved mystery in the game, the Eye Messages." So the same wiki simultaneously says (a) Eye Messages + Cauldron are 'the two major mod-free secrets' (Eye Messages page) and (b) there are THREE mod-restriction-hidden secrets and only ONE confirmed unsolved mystery, the Eye Messages (Cauldron page). The 'two' figure is therefore an editorial framing, not a stable, primary-sourced fact — and it is internally contradicted.
B. The Cauldron page already de-lists the Cauldron as an unsolved mystery, calling Eye Messages "the only confirmed unsolved mystery in the game." This directly undercuts the headline framing that there are TWO co-equal unsolved/major secrets; by the wiki's own admission today it is effectively one.
C. All of this is wiki/community framing (secondary, editor-written). The only developer primary source cited is Arvi (Nolla) in April 2021: "I'd maybe recommend not being too concerned about the cauldron for the time being." There is NO developer statement establishing a canonical 'two major secrets' list or confirming the Eye Messages encode solvable hidden information. The 'major secrets visible only in mod-free runs' construct is repeated community lore, not a primary/dev-confirmed taxonomy.

CONCLUSION: The verbatim quotes the claim relies on are accurate and the source attributions (which phrasing lives on which page) are correct. But the underlying assertion — that there are exactly 'two major secrets' that are the headline mod-free mysteries — is editorial, internally inconsistent (the wiki elsewhere says three mod-restricted secrets and only one confirmed unsolved mystery), and the Cauldron is now treated by the wiki as resolved/closed. Hence the claim is partially true (quotes and source mapping correct) but its central framing is overstated and contradicted within the same source. Mixed.

claim: The Noita eye puzzle is unsolved; simple monoalphabetic substitution is ruled out by flat trigram frequency. Stated evidence: ngraham20 README verbatim ('Frequency analysis has been tried on the trigrams, with no workable results, indicating that simple substitution isn't the answer'); Wiki/CodeWarrior0 ('The sample ciphertext frequency is flat, and not monoalphabetic'); FAQ ('Do we know what the eyes mean? A: No').
reasoning:
I tried hard to refute this and could not. All three cited quotes were verified verbatim against their primary sources, and every cross-check (a second wiki, Steam, the one solve-claim thread) reinforces rather than contradicts the claim.

VERIFICATION OF QUOTES:
1. ngraham20 README: Fetched directly from github.com/ngraham20/NoitaCryptographyResearch. Confirmed verbatim: "Frequency analysis has been tried on the trigrams, with no workable results, indicating that simple substition isn't the answer." (The original actually misspells "substitution" as "substition"; the claim paraphrases the spelling.) The README treats the puzzle as actively unsolved ("This is being currently explored" re: wheel/Alberti ciphers).
2. Wiki (noita.wiki.gg, attributed to CodeWarrior0's Analytical Overview): Confirmed verbatim: "The sample ciphertext frequency is flat, and not monoalphabetic," plus the corroborating "Period analysis shows the ciphertext is not periodic."
3. FAQ "Do we know what the eyes mean? A: No" confirmed as a documented community FAQ answer.

UNSOLVED STATUS: Independently corroborated by noita.wiki.gg, the Fandom mirror, and a moderator citing the wiki that "anyone claiming to have solved the eyes without presenting a method should not be believed." The only contradicting signal found, a Steam thread titled "i solved the eyes," is a dismissed hoax (OP refused any method/evidence; thread consensus rejected it). No credible public solution exists as of June 2026.

CONSISTENCY OF THE CIPHER ARGUMENT: The community independently concludes the cipher is POLYALPHABETIC (each ciphertext char depends on factors outside a single plaintext char; estimated ~20 to ~88 internal states; ciphertext-char dependence on the previous char), which is precisely the kind of structure that rules OUT simple monoalphabetic substitution. So the claim's logic is internally consistent with the broader documented analysis.

HONEST QUALIFICATIONS (why this is community-grade 'supported', not dev-confirmed fact):
- The inference "flat trigram frequency therefore not simple substitution" is sound cryptographic reasoning, but it is the community analysts' own conclusion, not a Nolla Games/Petri Purho statement nor a mathematically airtight proof. It is, however, the standard and correct diagnostic: a flat/uniform symbol distribution is inconsistent with a monoalphabetic substitution of a natural-language plaintext (which would inherit the plaintext's skewed frequencies).
- These summaries assert the frequency is "flat" but I did not see raw published frequency tables in the fetched text; the underlying data lives in CodeWarrior0's Analytical Overview / Toboter's Progress doc and the Discord work. The PRIMARY evidence here is community research documentation, not dev word or a reproducible game-file proof.
- The claim is compound; both halves (unsolved + monoalphabetic ruled out) are supported by the cited primary community documents and contradicted by none. Given strong agreement across multiple independent community sources and exact-quote verification, 'supported' is warranted, with the caveat that this is best-available community cryptanalysis rather than authoritative dev confirmation.

claim: Cryptanalysis of the Noita eye puzzle points to a polyalphabetic cipher with ~83 internal states, aperiodic, no doubled ciphertext letters, an anomalous distance-4 repetition, and a cipher/key constant across all messages — presented as working hypotheses by named researchers Toboter and CodeWarrior0, not proven.
reasoning:
The claim accurately reproduces what the cited wiki actually says, and the wiki's attributions and underlying primary sources are real — so as a description of "what these named researchers hypothesize," it is well-substantiated. As a description of "what the cipher actually is," it is unproven, and one sub-claim (the specific "83 internal states") is weaker than the rest. Breaking it down:

VERIFIED ATTRIBUTION / WIKI TEXT (high confidence). The raw wikitext of noita.wiki.gg/wiki/Eye_Messages matches the claim almost verbatim. Toboter's section states: "the cipher is polyalphabetic," "the cipher, key, and such outside factors are the same across all messages," and "the cipher has no fewer than 20 internal states and statistically probably at least around 88, so it is likely 83 internal states." CodeWarrior0's section states: "No ciphertext letter appears twice in a row" and "Ciphertext letters appear twice at a distance of 4, at nearly double the expected rate," plus "Period analysis shows the ciphertext is not periodic" (= aperiodic) and "The sample ciphertext frequency is flat, and not monoalphabetic." So every element of the claim is genuinely present and correctly attributed.

PRIMARY SOURCES EXIST (medium-high confidence). These are not merely repeated lore. The wiki links to two real primary documents: Toboter's "Progress Doc" (docs.google.com/document/d/1XMNXktCoSabnFWZf9rJFoaKMzsA1bbv7x1Xh9tXkKYk) and CodeWarrior0's "Analytical Overview" (docs.google.com/document/d/1QeagH8TklJsd8iribMtT5LIRL91laOUU_tFcVl7OOqA). CodeWarrior0 also maintains a companion code repo (github.com/codewarrior0/noita-eye-glyph-analyses) whose script names directly correspond to the claimed analyses: repeats.py (doubled-letter / distance-4 repetition), stat_period.py (periodicity), simple_freq.py (flat frequency), isomorphs.py, autokey_decrypt.py. Independent researchers (github.com/Azertinv/noita_eyes_cipher and cipher_fuzzer) have built their own toolchains, indicating the observations are reproducible from the ciphertext rather than asserted once.

CORRECTLY FRAMED AS HYPOTHESES (high confidence). The puzzle is unanimously described as UNSOLVED; the wiki and Steam discussion both state no public solution exists and that the cipher type is inferred, not confirmed. So the claim's own caveat ("working hypotheses, not proven") is accurate and important.

WHERE IT IS WEAKER / QUALIFIED (the reason for 'mixed' rather than 'supported'):
1) The "~83 internal states" figure is the softest element. The source itself is hedged and chained: "no fewer than 20 ... statistically probably at least around 88, so it is likely 83." The "83" is not derived as 83; it is a guess pinned to a separate finding (the accepted reading order is the only one yielding an unbroken base-10 set spanning 0–82, i.e. 83 values). So "83" is really "we suspect 83 because of the 0–82 set," a circumstantial inference, and even the lower bound "20" and central estimate "88" are statistical, not deductive. This part is closer to speculation than to an established measurement.
2) The "polyalphabetic," "aperiodic," "no doubled letters," and "distance-4 anomaly" items are stronger because they are direct statistical observations on the ciphertext — but I could NOT independently verify the underlying statistics: the two Google Docs are auth/render-gated and the doc bodies could not be retrieved, and I did not re-run the repos. So I am confirming that named researchers report these results with corresponding code, not that I personally reproduced the numbers. The "near double expected rate" for distance-4 has no error bar or significance test quoted in the secondary text.
3) Everything rests on the community's accepted reading order; if that reading order is wrong (it is itself a probabilistic argument, e.g. Toboter testing ~86,000 orderings), the downstream statistical properties could shift. No solid dev/Nolla primary statement confirms the cipher's nature; there is no developer confirmation of any of these properties.

Net: the claim is an accurate, faithfully-attributed report of real, code-backed working hypotheses by named researchers on a genuinely unsolved puzzle (supported as attribution), but the cipher properties themselves are unproven inferences and the specific "83 internal states" is a hedged guess (not supported as fact). That combination is properly 'mixed'.
